flashcards.front: What is the Gini impurity formula?
flashcards.back: Gini = 1 - SUM(p_i^2) where p_i is probability of class i. Range: 0 (pure) to 0.5 (binary, equal split).
Entropy = -SUM(p_i * log2(p_i)). Range: 0 (pure) to log2(K) for K classes.
Information Gain = Entropy(parent) - Weighted_Average(Entropy(children)). Higher gain = better split.

flashcards.front: What are the main parts of a decision tree?
flashcards.back: Root node (top), Internal nodes (feature tests), Branches (outcomes), Leaf nodes (predictions).
Setting constraints before/during tree construction: max_depth, min_samples_split, min_samples_leaf, max_features.
Growing a full tree first, then removing branches that don't improve validation performance (e.g., cost-complexity pruning).

flashcards.front: Why are decision trees prone to overfitting?
flashcards.back: Deep trees can create very specific rules that memorize training data. Each leaf may contain very few samples.

flashcards.front: What type of decision boundaries do decision trees create?
flashcards.back: Axis-parallel (orthogonal) boundaries. Each split is perpendicular to one feature axis.
